revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[az:Təhsil haqqında imperator reskripti]][[bg:Имперски декрет за образованието]][[de:Kaiserliches Erziehungsedikt]][[en:Imperial Rescript on Education]][[eo:Imperia Dekreto pri Edukado]][[es:Rescripto Imperial sobre la Educación]][[fa:متن امپراتوری در مورد آموزش]][[fr:Rescrit impérial sur l'éducation]][[id:Reskrip Kekaisaran tentang Pendidikan]][[it:Rescritto imperiale sull'educazione]][[ja:教育ニ関スル勅語]][[ko:교육에 관한 칙어]][[ru:Императорский рескрипт об образовании]][[tr:Eğitim Hakkında İmparatorluk Genelgesi]][[zh:教育敕語]] {{Short description|1890 edict in the Empire of Japan}} [[Image:50th Anniv.of Imperial Rescript on Education.jpg|right|300px|thumb|{{ill|Commemorative stamp|en|Commemorative stamp|qid=Q1756502|lt=Commemorative stamp|1=Commemorative stamp}}s celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Imperial Rescript in 1940]] The '''{{nihongo|Imperial Rescript on Education|教育ニ関スル勅語|Kyōiku ni Kansuru Chokugo}}''', or '''IRE''' for short, was signed by {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} of Japan on 30 October 1890 to articulate government policy on the guiding principles of education on the {{ill|Empire of Japan|en|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|lt=Empire of Japan|1=Empire of Japan}}. The 315 {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|qid=Q82772|lt=character|1=Kanji}} document was read aloud at all important school events, and students were required to study and memorize the text. ==Background== Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], the leadership of the {{ill|Government of Meiji Japan|en|Government of Meiji Japan|qid=Q1322181|lt=Meiji government|1=Government of Meiji Japan}} felt the need to emphasize the common goals of rapid modernization (westernization) with support and legitimization of the political system centered on the imperial institution. In the 1870s and 1880s, {{ill|Motoda Nagazane|en|Motoda Nagazane|qid=Q6533446|lt=Motoda Nagazane|1=Motoda Nagazane}} and other conservatives pushed for a revival of the principles of {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}} as a guide for education and {{ill|Public morality|en|Public morality|qid=Q108887968|lt=Public morality|1=Public morality}}; however, {{ill|Inoue Kowashi|en|Inoue Kowashi|qid=Q711131|lt=Inoue Kowashi|1=Inoue Kowashi}} and other proponents of the 'modernization' of Japan felt that this would encourage a return to the old feudal order, and pushed for an "emperor-centered" philosophy. {{ill|Prime Minister of Japan|en|Prime Minister of Japan|qid=Q274948|lt=Prime Minister|1=Prime Minister of Japan}} {{ill|Yamagata Aritomo|en|Yamagata Aritomo|qid=Q297636|lt=Yamagata Aritomo|1=Yamagata Aritomo}} authorized the drafting of the Rescript, which was a compromise written largely by Inoue Kowashi with input from Motoda Nagazane and others. After it was issued, the Rescript was distributed to all schools in the country, together with a [[portrait#Politics|portrait]] of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}}. == Text == {{Conservatism in Japan}} The text in {{ill|Classical Japanese|en|Classical Japanese|qid=Q1332057|lt=Classical Japanese|1=Classical Japanese}} reads:{{Cite web|last=sekicho|date=September 29, 2002|title=Imperial Rescript on Education|url=https://www.everything2.com/index.pl?node_id=1366527|website=Everything2}}
朕󠄁惟フニ我カ皇祖皇宗國ヲ肇󠄁ムルコト宏遠󠄁ニ德ヲ樹ツルコト深厚ナリ我カ臣民克ク忠ニ克ク孝ニ億兆心ヲ一ニシテ世世厥ノ美ヲ濟セルハ此レ我カ國體ノ精華ニシテ敎育ノ淵源亦實ニ此ニ存ス爾臣民父母ニ孝ニ兄弟ニ友ニ夫婦󠄁相和シ朋友相信シ恭儉己レヲ持シ博󠄁愛衆ニ及󠄁ホシ學ヲ修メ業ヲ習󠄁ヒ以テ智能ヲ啓󠄁發シ德器󠄁ヲ成就シ進󠄁テ公󠄁益󠄁ヲ廣メ世務ヲ開キ常ニ國憲ヲ重シ國法ニ遵󠄁ヒ一旦緩󠄁急󠄁アレハ義勇󠄁公󠄁ニ奉シ以テ天壤無窮󠄁ノ皇運󠄁ヲ扶翼󠄂スヘシ是ノ如キハ獨リ朕󠄁カ忠良ノ臣民タルノミナラス又󠄂以テ爾祖󠄁先ノ遺󠄁風ヲ顯彰スルニ足ラン 斯ノ道󠄁ハ實ニ我カ皇祖皇宗ノ遺󠄁訓ニシテ子孫臣民ノ俱ニ遵󠄁守スヘキ所󠄁之ヲ古今ニ通󠄁シテ謬ラス之ヲ中外ニ施シテ悖ラス朕󠄁爾臣民ト俱ニ拳󠄁々服󠄁膺シテ咸其德ヲ一ニセンコトヲ庶󠄂幾󠄁フ 明治二十三年十月三十日 '''御 名 御 璽'''
Translated into English:{{Cite web|title=Imperial Rescript On Education|url=https://www.japanpitt.pitt.edu/glossary/imperial-rescript-education|website=University of Pittsburgh - Japan}}
Know ye, Our subjects: Our Imperial Ancestors have founded Our Empire on a basis broad and everlasting and have deeply and firmly implanted virtue; Our subjects ever united in loyalty and filial piety have from generation to generation illustrated the beauty thereof. This is the glory of the fundamental character of Our Empire, and herein lies the source of Our education. Ye, Our subjects, be filial to your parents, affectionate to your brothers and sisters; as husbands and wives be harmonious; as friends true; bear yourselves in modesty and moderation; extend your benevolence to all; pursue learning and cultivate arts, and thereby develop intellectual faculties and perfect moral powers; furthermore advance public good and promote common interests; always respect the Constitution and observe the laws; should emergency arise, offer yourselves courageously to the State; and thus guard and maintain the prosperity of Our Imperial Throne coeval with heaven and earth. So shall ye not only be Our good and faithful subjects, but render illustrious the best traditions of your forefathers. The Way here set forth is indeed the teaching bequeathed by Our Imperial Ancestors, to be observed alike by Their Descendants and the subjects, infallible for all ages and true in all places. It is Our wish to lay it to heart in all reverence, in common with you, Our subjects, that we may thus attain to the same virtue. ''The 30th day of the 10th month of the 23rd year of Meiji.'' '''(Imperial Sign Manual. Imperial Seal.)'''
==Details== The Rescript pushed traditional ideals of {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}, and in many ways contributed to the rise of militarism during the 1930s and 40s. The Rescript requested of the people that they "furthermore advance public good and promote common interests; always respect the {{ill|Meiji Constitution|en|Meiji Constitution|qid=Q122804|lt=Constitution|1=Meiji Constitution}} and observe the laws; should emergency arise, offer yourselves courageously to the State; and thus guard and maintain the prosperity of Our Imperial Throne coeval with heaven and earth". The basis of the Rescript was that Japan's unique ''[[kokutai]]'' (system of government) was based on a historic bond between benevolent rulers and loyal subjects, and that the fundamental purpose of education was to cultivate virtues, especially loyalty and {{ill|Filial piety|en|Filial piety|qid=Q614263|lt=Filial piety|1=Filial piety}}. After the {{ill|End of World War II in Asia|en|End of World War II in Asia|qid=Q5375854|lt=End of World War II in Asia|1=End of World War II in Asia}} following the {{ill|Surrender of Japan|en|Surrender of Japan|qid=Q6540361|lt=Surrender of Japan|1=Surrender of Japan}}, the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|American occupation authorities]] forbade the formal reading of the Imperial Rescript in schools, and the {{ill|National Diet|en|National Diet|qid=Q202384|lt=National Diet|1=National Diet}} officially abolished it on 19 June 1948. ==Kikuchi Dairoku and the Imperial Rescript on Education== In 1907, {{ill|Kikuchi Dairoku|en|Kikuchi Dairoku|qid=Q976582|lt=Kikuchi Dairoku|1=Kikuchi Dairoku}} was invited by the {{ill|University of London|en|University of London|qid=Q170027|lt=University of London|1=University of London}} to give lectures on education from the middle of February for about five months. The central focus of his lectures was the Imperial Rescript on Education. The request for the lectures was initially communicated to {{ill|Hayashi Tadasu|en|Hayashi Tadasu|qid=Q1138734|lt=Hayashi Tadasu|1=Hayashi Tadasu}}, then ambassador in {{ill|en:London|en|en:London|qid=Q84|lt=en:London|1=London}} (from December 1905). At first [[Sawayanagi Matsutaro]] was to give the lectures, but he was recalled when in [[Rome]] on the way to London and Kikuchi gave the lectures instead. As a preparation for the lectures he translated the Imperial Rescript into English. ==See also== {{Wikisource|Imperial Rescript on Education}} {{Commonscat|}} * {{ill|Education in the Empire of Japan|en|Education in the Empire of Japan|qid=Q3509524|lt=Education in the Empire of Japan|1=Education in the Empire of Japan}} * [[:en:Ho an den|Ho an den]]: A temple or shrine-like small building that housed the Imperial Rescript on Education, together with a photograph of the Emperor and the Empress. * ''{{ill|Shotouka-Chiri|en|Shotouka-Chiri|qid=Q7502562|lt=Shotouka-Chiri|1=Shotouka-Chiri}}'' official geography text ==Sources== * ''[https://web.archive.org/web/20090217225922/http://www.dhs.kyutech.ac.jp/~ruxton/hatenkou.html Japanese Students at Cambridge University in the Meiji Era, 1868–1912: Pioneers for the Modernization of Japan]'', by Noboru Koyama, translated by Ian Ruxton September 2004, {{ISBN|1-4116-1256-6}}). ==References== {{Reflist}} {{JapanEmpireNavbox}} {{Authority control}} [[en:Imperial Rescript on Education]] {{wikidata link|Q712830}} [[Category:1890 in Japan]] [[Category:Education laws and guidelines in Japan]] [[Category:Emperor Meiji]] [[Category:Government of the Empire of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Imperial rescripts]] [[Category:Rescripts]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 100+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 150+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 200+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 250+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with 300+ untranslated japanese characters]]
